title: How to improve pixelpack encoding ratio from 7:1 to 20:1+
description: Each 32-bit pixel expands to ~4-5 bytes. We need strategies that extract more entropy from the 28 param bits.

children.title: Context-Dependent Expansion
children.description: Pixel N can reference pixel N-1 output. A pixel with strategy CTX_COPY copies the last K bytes from the previous expansion and appends new bytes. This means repeated patterns (indentation, variable names) cost 0 bits after first occurrence.
enables: Back-references eliminate redundant bytes across pixels
Run-length patterns in code (indentation, repeated keywords) become nearly free
Enables fractal unfold: each pixels output feeds into next pixels context
children.risk: Breaks independent pixel decoding -- must decode sequentially. Makes PNG order matter.

children.title: LZ77-style back-ref strategy
children.description: Strategy bit=0xC means: params encode (offset, length) back-reference into the running output buffer. 14 bits offset + 14 bits length = copy up to 16KB back, 16KB length.
enables: Replaces repeated substrings with 32-bit pixels
Could encode 200-byte programs with many repeated tokens in ~10 pixels
children.risk: Need a running buffer during decode. More complex state.

children.title: Delta encoding between pixels
children.description: Pixel N stores XOR deltas from pixel N-1 output. If consecutive code segments share prefixes (like "    " indentation), the delta is tiny and the pixel can store mostly literal new content.
enables: Code with consistent indentation compresses very well
Natural for multi-line programs
children.risk: One corrupted pixel cascades. Need error detection.

children.title: Shared dictionary built during decode
children.description: As each pixel expands, common byte sequences get added to a dynamic dictionary (like LZ78/LZW). Later pixels can reference dictionary entries by short index.
enables: Dictionary adapts to the specific program being encoded
Common patterns like "def ", "return ", "print(" become single references after first use
children.risk: Dictionary state must be deterministic. Different programs build different dicts.

children.title: Chained Huffman per-pixel
children.description: Each pixel re-encodes its output using a Huffman table derived from the frequency distribution of all previous pixels output. Later pixels use shorter codes for bytes seen frequently so far.
enables: Adaptive compression that learns the programs own patterns
Could approach entropy limit for repetitive code
children.risk: Complex. Huffman table itself needs encoding. May not fit in 28 param bits.

children.title: Variable-Length Dictionary Indices
children.description: Current strategies use fixed-width indices (4-bit or 5-bit) into dictionaries. Waste bits on common entries. Use variable-length: top 8 entries get 3-bit indices, next 16 get 5-bit, etc. Fits more dictionary lookups into the same 28 param bits.
enables: More dictionary hits per pixel = more bytes per pixel
Common tokens (print, def, return, spaces) use fewer bits
Can pack 8-10 lookups instead of 5-7 into one pixel
children.risk: Parsing variable-length fields from a 28-bit word is tricky. Need unambiguous bit boundaries.

children.title: Huffman-coded dictionary index
children.description: Build a frequency table from corpus. Assign shorter bit codes to more common dictionary entries. 28 param bits become a Huffman-coded bitstream of dictionary lookups.
enables: Near-optimal use of the 28 param bits
Could fit 10+ common tokens in one pixel
children.risk: Need to store or agree on the Huffman table. Slight decode complexity.

children.title: Prefix code with escape
children.description: First bit = 0 means 3-bit index into top-4 common entries. First bit = 1 means next 6 bits = index into 64-entry dictionary. Hybrid: common stuff is cheap, rare stuff is possible.
enables: Common tokens cost 4 bits, rare ones cost 7 bits
Simple to decode, no table needed
children.risk: Still not as efficient as full Huffman. Fixed breakpoint may not match actual distribution.

children.title: Arithmetic Coding Strategy
children.description: Replace one strategy slot with arithmetic coding: the 28 param bits encode a fractional number that, decoded against a known symbol probability table, produces a variable-length sequence of bytes. Much denser than dictionary lookup.
enables: 28 bits can represent any sequence up to ~28 bits of entropy
Near Shannon limit for the given symbol distribution
Could encode 10-20 bytes per pixel for structured code
children.risk: Need a pre-agreed probability model. Slower decode. More complex implementation.

children.title: Static model from corpus
children.description: Build a byte-frequency model from the training corpus. 28 bits encode a range that decodes to the most likely byte sequence for that range.
enables: Deterministic, no metadata needed in pixel
Good for programs in the same language as training corpus
children.risk: Static model is suboptimal for programs that deviate from corpus distribution.

children.title: Model-switching per strategy
children.description: Strategy 0xD = Python model, 0xE = C model, 0xF = shell model. Each strategy uses a different arithmetic coding probability table optimized for that language.
enables: Language-specific compression without metadata
Common Python tokens (def, import, class) get optimal encoding in 0xD, C tokens (int, void, return) in 0xE
children.risk: Uses 3 of 16 strategy slots. Limited to 3 languages unless we expand strategy bits.

children.title: Expand Nibble Table to Full Byte Map
children.description: Current NIBBLE strategy (0x7) maps 7 nibbles to 7 bytes via a 16-entry common-symbol table. Expand: use ALL 28 param bits as 7 nibbles each selecting from a 256-entry table (using 8-bit indices packed differently). Or better: use the 28 bits to encode offsets into a larger frequency-sorted byte-pair table.
enables: 7 bytes per pixel using any byte, not just 16 common ones
Byte-pair table means each nibble emits 2 bytes = 14 bytes per pixel
children.risk: Nibble math constrains flexibility. 4 bits cant index 256 entries without multiplexing.

children.title: Byte-pair nibble strategy
children.description: 16-entry table of the most common 2-byte pairs in code. Each nibble selects a pair. 7 nibbles = 14 bytes. Ratio: 14*8/32 = 3.5:1 bits, much better than current.
enables: Double the output per pixel
Common pairs like \n\t, ()\n, ": ", "= " become single nibbles
children.risk: Need to identify the 16 most valuable byte pairs. May not cover all needed characters.

children.title: Meta-Pixel: Strategy Selects Strategy
children.description: A meta-strategy pixel that doesnt produce output directly but modifies how subsequent pixels are decoded. Example: pixel with strategy META sets the dictionary to Python-mode, and all following pixels use Python-optimized tables until another META pixel switches.
enables: One pixel to configure the entire decoding context
Language-specific modes without wasting strategy slots
Future-proof: new modes dont need new strategies, just new META payloads
children.risk: Breaks the purity of each pixel being independent. Adds state machine to decoder.

children.title: Mode-switch + dictionary swap
children.description: META pixel payload selects one of N pre-built dictionaries. Following pixels use that dictionary. Switch dictionaries mid-program for polyglot code.
enables: Optimal dictionary for each section of code
Python preamble + C inline in same image
children.risk: Must track current mode during decode. More state = more test surface.
